danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring avoidance or combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Toxic/polluted visual elements in level design
danger_signals.meaning: Thematic danger aligned with game's toxic/ecological premise

danger_signals.visual_form: Larger sprite scaling, distinct animation patterns
danger_signals.meaning: Major world-state threat at level completion

safety_signals.visual_form: Sprite collection animation and inventory change

safety_signals.meaning: Temporary immunity to damage — world-state of safety

safety_signals.visual_form: Stage completion animation, transition to next area

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Playable character sprite identity; weapon power visual progression

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinct sprite sets per enemy type; variety suggests multiple enemy factions
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Level-specific enemy placements; boss sprites signal faction leaders

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Stage transitions; cutscene viewer shows narrative progression via demo sequences
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy behavior and placement changes per level; difficulty escalation

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Sprite palette and attack animation changes reflect power tier
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Attack range, damage, and projectile behavior scale with power level

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Moon color changes from default to red under specific input (Up+A+B Controller 2)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Environmental storytelling Easter egg — suggests hidden world-state mechanics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character sprite rendering may change (debug feature)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Collision damage disabled; enemies cannot harm player

environmental_storytelling.example: Game world visually designed around toxic/ecological hazards; enemy design reflects mutation/contamination
Demo sequences communicate story context between levels; message viewer reveals dialogue and plot exposition
Final level (Level 6) boss sprite signals climax of narrative arc and world threat escalation
Varied enemy sprite distribution per stage suggests environmental storytelling through inhabitant types
Red moon Easter egg in Level 2 reveals hidden world mechanics and suggests dynamic environmental states

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Platforming movement, attack response to input, weapon fire
Movement unchanged; collision damage negated; sprite may render differently
Collision-based threat; variable animation and movement patterns per enemy type
Larger scale; distinct animation set; higher health and attack complexity signal major threat

entity_behavioral_tells.visual_form: Red color palette swap signals hidden world mechanic activation

notes: Toxic Crusaders uses limited NES palette and sprite-based communication. World-state is primarily conveyed through enemy placement, cutscene progression, weapon power visual feedback, and discrete level transitions. The game lacks complex faction politics; conflict is binary (Crusaders vs. Toxic enemies). Environmental storytelling relies on thematic visual design (toxic/ecological) rather than detailed world-building. Debug features reveal intended but unused password system that would have saved weapon power state — suggesting originally planned save-state communication system. Easter egg red moon in Level 2 indicates developers embedded hidden world-state mechanics. Unused 'mutant girl head' graphic suggests cut content related to faction or enemy design.
sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor: Toxic Crusaders NES)
